question: Using Table 1.3-1 (design life 60 vs 40 years) and Table 5.3-3 (ART at 57 EFPY), is the longer NuScale design life justified?

nuscale_ch05_0015.title: Reactor Vessel Materials
nuscale_ch05_0015.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module’s reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a containment vessel. The RPV is a key component of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, designed and fabricated with materials specified by the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code to ensure integrity and safety throughout the plant’s operational lifetime.

nuscale_ch05_0017.title: Reactor Vessel Integrity
nuscale_ch05_0017.summary: This section outlines the procedures and regulatory compliance measures in place to ensure the integrity of the NuScale reactor vessel throughout its 60-year design life. It details the development of operating procedures to maintain pressure-temperature limits, the evaluation of pressurized thermal shock using NRC-approved methodologies, and the assurance that reactor vessel materials retain adequate toughness as required by federal regulations.

nuscale_ch01_0558.title: DSRS 12.3-12.4, Rev 0: Radiation Protection Design FeaturesII.9 RG 8.15
nuscale_ch01_0558.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with various regulatory guides related to radiation protection design features, as outlined in DSRS 12.3-12.4, Rev 0. It details whether each regulatory guide is applicable, and if so, whether NuScale’s design fully or partially conforms, with references to supporting sections or tables for further information. Several guides are noted as not applicable, while others are partially or fully met.

nuscale_ch01_0052.title: Comparison with Other Facilities
nuscale_ch01_0052.summary: This section compares the major design features and parameters of the NuScale Power Plant with those of a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR). It provides a side-by-side summary of key characteristics such as electrical output, core design, reactor coolant system, and safety components, highlighting the differences in scale, configuration, and safety system requirements between NuScale and conventional PWRs. All values are nominal and intended for comparison purposes only.

nuscale_ch05_0018.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0018.summary: This section outlines the inspection, shipment, installation, operating conditions, inservice surveillance, and threaded fastener requirements for the NuScale reactor vessel, referencing relevant ASME and ASTM standards. It specifies procedures for maintaining vessel integrity during transport and operation, and lists the key codes and standards used for compliance.

nuscale_ch01_0049.title: Central Utility Building
nuscale_ch01_0049.summary: The Central Utility Building (CUB) is a non-safety-related structure that houses essential utility services for the plant, such as chiller equipment, air systems, water treatment, maintenance, and security functions. The design also includes Diesel Generator Buildings (DGBs) for backup power, which are not safety-related, and emphasizes human factors engineering to minimize errors and optimize control room operations through automation and digital technologies.

nuscale_ch01_0050.title: Diesel Generator Buildings
nuscale_ch01_0050.summary: The Diesel Generator Buildings (DGBs) in the NuScale Power Plant each house a single backup diesel generator and their auxiliary equipment, providing support and housing but containing no safety-related systems or functions supporting engineered safety features (ESFs). The DGBs include diesel engines, generators, HVAC systems, and maintenance areas.

nuscale_ch05_0016.title: Pressure-Temperature Limits, Pressurized Thermal Shock, and Charpy Upper-Shelf Energy Data and Analyses
nuscale_ch05_0016.summary: This section outlines the basis for establishing operational pressure and temperature limits for the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) to comply with regulatory requirements over the plant’s 60-year lifespan. It describes the methodology used to calculate pressure-temperature limit curves, accounting for vessel embrittlement, and provides figures and tables with these limits to ensure protection against non-ductile failure.

nuscale_ch01_0419.title: DSRS 10.2.3, Rev 0: Turbine Rotor IntegrityII.2 Fracture Toughness
nuscale_ch01_0419.summary: This section explains that the NuScale plant design uses physical barriers to protect safety-related structures, systems, and components (SSCs) from turbine missile hazards. As a result, requirements related to turbine rotor integrity, including fracture toughness, materials selection, and inspections, are not applicable because the plant does not rely on turbine missile generation probabilities for safety.

nuscale_ch05_0020.title: Steam Generators
nuscale_ch05_0020.summary: The steam generators (SGs) in the NuScale Power Module are integrated within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which also houses other key components such as the pressurizer, safety valves, and emergency cooling systems. The SG system includes feedwater and main steam piping, thermal relief valves, and access ports, with the RPV itself forming the outer pressure boundary for the SGs and contributing to the reactor coolant pressure boundary through the generator tubes and welds.

nuscale_ch01_0430.title: SRP 10.3.6, Rev 3: Steam and Feedwater System MaterialsII.2 Fracture Toughness of Class 2 and 3 Components
nuscale_ch01_0430.summary: This section states that the NuScale design does not contain any Class 2 or 3 components, so fracture toughness requirements for these classes are not applicable. For other systems, the NuScale design generally conforms to regulatory criteria, with some exceptions or departures noted where the design uses NuScale-specific criteria or where requirements apply only to BWRs. The section also highlights differences in secondary water chemistry requirements due to the unique NuScale steam generator design.

nuscale_ch01_0477.title: DSRS 11.3, Rev 0: Gaseous Waste Management SystemII.3 Seismic Design and Quality Group Classification of Components and Structures Housing Gaseous Waste Management System
nuscale_ch01_0477.summary: This section confirms that the seismic design and quality group classification of components and structures housing the Gaseous Waste Management System fully conforms to regulatory requirements, with no exceptions noted. The relevant criteria are addressed in section 11.3.1 of the report.

nuscale_ch01_0484.title: DSRS 11.3, Rev 0: Gaseous Waste Management SystemII.10 Relevant RGs, ISG, and BTP
nuscale_ch01_0484.summary: This section summarizes the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report’s conformance with regulatory criteria for the Gaseous Waste Management System (DSRS 11.3, Rev 0). Most criteria are met, with partial conformance noted for dose design objectives and features minimizing contamination, as some site-specific responsibilities are deferred to the COL applicant. The report details compliance for system design, seismic classification, filtration, automatic controls, and safety features.

nuscale_ch05_0021.title: Reactor Coolant System Piping
nuscale_ch05_0021.summary: The section discusses the design and degradation assessment of the NuScale reactor coolant system's steam generator (SG) tubing. It notes that the main degradation observed in similar operating reactors is wear from flow-induced vibration or foreign objects, and that NuScale's design—with its helical tubing and novel support structure—does not increase risk compared to existing designs. Prototypic testing and standard SG program guidelines are deemed sufficient, with wear remaining the most likely degradation mechanism and corrosion risks assessed as low.

edges.description: Section A describes the physical design and materials of the reactor vessel, which directly impact the vessel's ability to maintain integrity over the design life discussed in Section B, where operating procedures are established to ensure the vessel's pressure-temperature limits (and thus integrity) are not exceeded throughout its operational period.

Section B (Table 1.3-1) provides the design life values (60 vs 40 years) for NuScale and typical PWRs, while Section A describes the reactor vessel materials relevant to assessing whether the vessel can meet the longer design life, thus connecting the design life comparison to the technical basis for vessel longevity.

Section B (Table 1.3-1) provides the design life values (60 vs 40 years) for NuScale and typical PWRs, while Section A addresses the operational procedures and technical specifications (including ART at 57 EFPY) that ensure vessel integrity over the design life, thus connecting the design life comparison to the technical justification for the longer NuScale design life.

Section B's Table 1.3-1 provides the design life value (60 vs 40 years) for NuScale and typical PWRs, which is a key parameter that must be justified by demonstrating that material properties (such as ART at 57 EFPY from Table 5.3-3) remain within acceptable limits, as required by the regulatory conformance discussed in Section A.

Section A describes the inspection, shipment, and storage requirements for the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), referencing standards that ensure its integrity over time, while Section B provides a general description of the RPV's role and configuration, thus Section A specifies detailed requirements for the general system described in Section B.

Section A describes inspection and handling requirements for the reactor pressure vessel, while Section B discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure compliance with pressure-temperature limits; both sections are connected through their roles in maintaining reactor vessel integrity over the design life, which is central to justifying the longer design life in the analysis question.

Section A details the inspection, handling, and storage requirements for the reactor pressure vessel, which are necessary to ensure the vessel's integrity over the design life discussed in Table 1.3-1, while Section B outlines the NuScale FSAR's conformance with radiation protection regulatory criteria, which must be satisfied for the longer design life to be justified.

Section A discusses compliance of specific welds and attachments with ASME code requirements for the reactor pressure vessel, while Section B provides a general description of the reactor vessel and its materials, with Section A specifying details for the general vessel described in Section B.

Section A discusses the technical compliance of reactor vessel welds with ASME code requirements, while Section B addresses the development of operating procedures to ensure pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded, both of which are necessary to justify the longer design life by demonstrating vessel integrity over time.

Section A describes inspection and handling requirements for the reactor pressure vessel per ASME codes, while Section B details specific weld examination and code compliance for attachments, both providing evidence that the vessel's fabrication and inspection processes adhere to regulatory standards relevant to justifying the longer design life.

Section A discusses regulatory conformance for turbine rotor integrity and fracture toughness, while Section B describes the reactor vessel materials and design, with both sections providing information relevant to the justification of the NuScale design life by addressing different safety-critical components (turbine vs. reactor vessel) and their compliance with regulatory requirements.

Section A discusses the regulatory conformance and fracture toughness requirements for turbine rotor integrity, while Section B addresses the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel integrity under pressure-temperature limits; both sections are connected through their focus on maintaining structural integrity and safety over the plant's design life, which is central to justifying the longer NuScale design life.

Section A describes the overall design and components of the NuScale reactor coolant system, including the steam generators and reactor pressure vessel, while Section B provides specific details about the reactor vessel materials and configuration, with both sections connecting through their discussion of the reactor pressure vessel's design and its implications for design life justification.

Section A describes the design features and components of the NuScale reactor, including the steam generators, which are relevant to the plant's intended 60-year design life (from Table 1.3-1), while Section B discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel integrity under pressure-temperature limits, which is necessary to justify safe operation up to the ART at 57 EFPY (from Table 5.3-3); thus, the design features in A must support the operational requirements in B to justify the longer design life.

Section A describes the design and components of the NuScale reactor coolant system, including steam generators, while Section B lists the regulatory guidance and the degree of conformance for radiation protection features, which are relevant for justifying the design life extension by ensuring continued regulatory compliance.

Section A establishes that the NuScale design does not include Class 2 or 3 components relevant to fracture toughness requirements, while Section B describes the reactor vessel as a pressure-retaining component, connecting them by clarifying which material requirements apply to the vessel in the context of design life justification.

Section B provides information on the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel integrity over the design life, which supports the justification for the longer design life referenced in Table 1.3-1, as required by the regulatory criteria in Section A.

Section A and Section B both present NuScale's conformance with regulatory criteria, but Section A addresses material and system requirements while Section B addresses radiation protection features, and neither provides direct information on design life or ART values, so they are only structurally related as parallel regulatory conformance summaries.

Section A and Section B both document the NuScale FSAR's conformance with regulatory criteria, with Section A focusing on waste management system components and Section B on radiation protection design features, and both reference specific regulatory guides and their applicability or partial conformance, thus providing complementary evidence regarding the plant's ability to meet safety requirements over the proposed design life.

Section A discusses regulatory conformance and acceptance criteria for waste management systems, while Section B describes the reactor vessel's design and materials; both sections are connected through the regulatory requirements that the reactor vessel (Section B) must satisfy to justify the longer design life referenced in Section A.

Section B discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel integrity over the plant's operational life, which is directly connected to the design life values (60 vs 40 years) referenced in Table 1.3-1 and the ART at 57 EFPY in Table 5.3-3, thus providing evidence relevant to justifying the longer design life mentioned in Section A.

Section A and Section B both document the NuScale FSAR's conformance with regulatory criteria, with Section A focusing on waste management systems and Section B on radiation protection design features, thus together providing evidence relevant to whether the design life extension (from 40 to 60 years) maintains compliance with applicable regulatory requirements.

Section A discusses the operational wear mechanisms and design considerations for steam generator tubing over the design life, while Section B describes the reactor vessel as a key pressure boundary component whose material properties (such as ART at 57 EFPY) are critical for justifying an extended design life, thus both sections provide complementary technical bases for evaluating whether the longer NuScale design life is justified.

Section A discusses the design and degradation mechanisms of steam generator tubing over the design life, while Section B addresses the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel integrity over the plant's operational period; both sections are connected through their focus on maintaining component integrity and safety over the extended design life (60 vs 40 years) as referenced in the analysis question.

Section A outlines regulatory criteria and conformance for turbine rotor integrity and fracture toughness, while Section B describes the design and components of the reactor coolant system, including steam generators; both sections are connected through the need to ensure that material properties and inspection requirements (such as fracture toughness and ART at end-of-life) justify the extended design life of 60 years versus 40 years.

Section A and Section B both address fracture toughness requirements for different components, but both conclude that the specific regulatory criteria for fracture toughness are not applicable to the NuScale design due to the absence of relevant components (turbine missiles and Class 2/3 components, respectively), thus providing consistent justification for the design approach regardless of design life.

Section A discusses regulatory conformance and fracture toughness requirements for turbine rotor integrity, while Section B provides operational experience and degradation mechanisms for steam generator tubing, both of which are connected by the need to justify the longer design life (60 vs 40 years) in terms of material integrity and safety compliance.

Section A describes the design and components of the NuScale reactor coolant system, including steam generators, while Section B documents the regulatory conformance of these components, specifically noting that certain fracture toughness requirements for Class 2 and 3 components are not applicable because the NuScale design contains no such components.

Section B provides operational experience and degradation mechanisms for steam generator tubing materials, which supports the justification for the longer design life (60 years) referenced in Section A by showing that the NuScale design is not at greater risk than existing designs.

Section B provides operational experience and degradation mechanisms for steam generator tubing materials, which supports the justification in Section A for the NuScale design's conformance with regulatory criteria over an extended design life.

Section A and Section B both present conformance evaluations of the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report against DSRS 11.3 requirements, with Section B specifically listing relevant regulatory guides and criteria referenced in Section A's conformance statements.

Section B provides evidence regarding the durability and degradation mechanisms of steam generator tubing, which supports the justification for the longer design life discussed in Section A by showing no increased risk compared to existing designs.

Section A references regulatory criteria and conformance requirements that are relevant for evaluating the design life justification, while Section B provides specific design and operational experience data (e.g., wear mechanisms and risk comparisons) that serve as evidence to support whether the longer design life is justified.
